content:
Let 1, 11, 111 be the only allowed “blocks,” since 1111=1111>1000.  Writing

 x·1 + y·11 + z·111 = 1000,

we get x=1000−11y−111z ≥0, and the total length is

 n = x + 2y + 3z = (1000−11y−111z) + 2y + 3z
  = 1000 − 9y − 108z.

For each z=0,1,…,9 (since 111·10>1000) one finds 0≤y≤⌊(1000−111z)/11⌋, and hence the possible n form arithmetic progressions

 n = 1000−108z − 9y, y=0,1,…,⌊(1000−111z)/11⌋ .

One checks that as z runs from 9 down to 0 these intervals of integers (all ≡1 mod 9)

  – for z=9 gives n=28;  
  – for z=8 gives n=136, 127,…,46;  
  – for z=7 gives 244,…,64;  
  …  
  – for z=0 gives 1000, 991,…,190;  

and that their union is exactly {28} together with all n≡1 mod 9 in the range 46≤n≤1000.  Since 46≡1 mod 9 and 1000≡1 mod 9, there are

 ( (1000−46)/9 +1 ) = 106+1 =107

such n between 46 and 1000, plus the n=28, for a total of 107+1=108 values of n.  Hence

 a = 108,

and the sum of the digits of a is

 1 + 0 + 8 = 9.

Answer: \boxed{9}.
